name: engineering-management
description: Codex skills for engineering managers and team leads — covering the tactical day-to-day mechanics (first-week snapshot, manager toolkit, 1-1s, feedback and reviews, hiring, motivation, stress and pressure, information and politics, self-care, team health, career growth) plus the longer-arc and cross-cutting work (project breakdown, hard conversations, the annual career vision exercise, and turning unspoken expectations into explicit agreements). Derived from James Stanier's Become an Effective Software Engineering Manager, Camille Fournier's The Manager's Path, and Niels Horeman's Leadership Essentials series.

interface.shortDescription: Skills for managing engineers: 1-1s, feedback, hiring, project planning, team health, hard conversations, career growth, agreements, and self-organization.
interface.longDescription: A Codex plugin that bundles reusable skills for the daily and longer-arc work of engineering management: the first-week snapshot for new managers, the manager toolkit (calendar, to-do, email, capture), 1-1s with contracting and rolling agendas, giving feedback and writing performance reviews with no surprises, hiring engineers and managers (loop design, JDs, take-homes, debriefs, references, calibration), motivation and fit, project breakdown and estimation, team health (Conway's Law, guilds, ADRs, five whys, health checks, DRIs — plus diagnosing dysfunctional teams), hard conversations (PIPs, coaching out, saying no upward), stress and pressure handling, information sharing and politics, self-care, career growth including the 10-year vision exercise, and turning unspoken expectations into explicit, mutual, voluntary agreements. Skills are grounded in James Stanier's Become an Effective Software Engineering Manager, Camille Fournier's The Manager's Path, and Niels Horeman's Leadership Essentials series.
defaultPrompt: Use engineering-management skills to help me lead my team this week.
Use first-week-snapshot to structure my first week as the new manager of this team.
Use manager-toolkit to audit how I spend my time and clean up my inputs.
Use one-on-ones to run a contracting session with my new direct report.
Use one-on-ones to prep this week's 1-1 with this report.
Use one-on-ones to extract follow-ups from these 1-1 notes.
Use motivation-and-fit to figure out what would re-engage this report.
Use feedback-and-reviews to set up the review tracker for the next cycle.
Use feedback-and-reviews to plan how to give this piece of feedback.
Use hiring-and-interviewing to design an interview loop for this role.
Use hiring-and-interviewing to debrief and calibrate after this onsite.
Use project-breakdown to break down this multi-week project into milestones.
Use project-breakdown to decide what to cut as the deadline approaches.
Use team-health to diagnose why my team feels off, or to install a healthy practice.
Use hard-conversations to prepare for telling this engineer they are not getting promoted.
Use hard-conversations to plan how to say no to this leadership ask.
Use stress-and-pressure to plan what to cut as this deadline approaches.
Use information-and-politics to decide what I can share with the team about this.
Use letting-go-and-self-care to help me stop thinking about work in the evening.
Use career-growth to build a growth plan for this engineer.
Use career-growth to evaluate whether this engineer is ready for the next level.
Use career-growth to deliver a 10-year career conversation to this senior engineer.
Use agreements-not-expectations to turn this unspoken expectation into a clear agreement.
Use agreements-not-expectations to draft the out-of-agreement conversation after this missed deadline.
